item.title: He delivers
item.description: Damn straight. Being a single dad, I explain that housekeeping is all our jobs. That we both chip in to do the chores that have to be done. It was like this when his mother was alive and still today. One of my proudest moments was last year he went to a friends for a sleepover/play video games/etc. The mother of the friend called me the next day and told me what a well mannered, polite boy he is and how he helped clean the table after dinner. My smile could have lit up the entire neighborhood.
item.dc:creator: Herebychoice

item.title: She's done
item.description:
Finished up painting this 3D printed mask. Just going to hit it with some shadows from the airbrush. Had fun painting this one and it feels good to see a final product.
Loving the gold File designer by Pipe Cox Printed on an FLSun V400
item.dc:creator: vudumommajuju

item.title: A Mega-Dump of Music from the '80s Airwaves
item.description:
The Bangles - Walk Like an Egyptian A handy dandy Spotify playlist for this Mega Dump - https://open.spotify.com/playlist/0GPBvTbhOo8QmXHmhtiAoN?si=013d3d1996734285
Bobby McFerrin - Don't Worry, Be Happy
Madonna - Lucky Star
Taylor Dane - Tell It To My Heart
Falco - Rock Me Amadeus
Belinda Carlisle - Heaven is a Place on Earth
Dead or Alive - You Spin Me Round (Like a Record)
The GoGos - Vacation
Harold Faltermeyer - Axel F
Sinéad O'Connor - Nothing Compares 2 U
Eurythmics - Would I Lie to You?
Toto - Africa
Joan Jett (and the Blackhearts) - I Hate Myself for Loving You
Billy Joel - Uptown Girl
Tracy Chapman - Fast Car
Beastie Boys - No Sleep Til Brooklyn
Madonna - Into the Groove
Queen - One Vision
OPUS - Live is Life
Cyndi Lauper - Girls Just Wanna Have Fun (EDIT: A handy dandy Spotify playlist for this collection https://open.spotify.com/playlist/0zx1WMC0oJD2nRwSOrBU4T ) The hit songs from 30 years ago, Wayback in 1993 - https://imgur.com/gallery/tuAN3R4
Huey Lewis and the News - Back in Time
Eddy Grant - Electric Avenue
WHAM - Wake Me Up (Before You Go Go)
Simple Minds - Don't You (Forget About Me)
Dolly Parton and Kenny Rogers - Islands in the Stream
Billy Idol - White Wedding
Sheena Easton - The Lover in Me
Dexys Midnight Runner - Come on Eileen
Bruce Springsteen - Dancing in the Dark
Whitney Houston - So Emotional
The Clash - Rock the Casbah
Elton John - I'm Still Standing
Tina Turner - We Don't Need Another Hero
Enya - Orinoco Flow
Pink Floyd - Another Brick in the Wall (Part 2)
aha - Take on Me
Thomas Dolby - She Blinded Me With Science
Huey Lewis and the News - The Power of Love
George Michael - Faith
Toni Basil - Mickey
Madonna - Holiday
Chris Issak - Wicked Game
The Pointer Sisters - Neutron Dance

Dire Straits - Money for Nothing
RUN DMC - Mary Mary
Katrina and the Waves - Walking on Sunshine
Culture Club - Karma Chameleon
Duran Duran - The Wild Boys
Michael Jackson - Bllie Jean
Debbie Harry - Call Me A handy dandy Spotify playlist for this collection - https://open.spotify.com/playlist/3AjwzxqE0t0vi8D92q7q5r Wayback in 73 - https://imgur.com/gallery/2rlT8Yh Wayback in 83 - https://imgur.com/gallery/T1CGaYx Wayback in 85 - https://imgur.com/gallery/lrddmXp Wayback in 88 - https://imgur.com/gallery/qBqeSu1 Wayback in 93 - https://imgur.com/gallery/tuAN3R4 Wayback in 95 - https://imgur.com/gallery/KkckKb3 Wayback in 98 - https://imgur.com/gallery/2qki0gA Wayback in 03 - https://imgur.com/gallery/pyMZXVP

DeBarge - Rhythm of the Night
Glenn Frey - The Heat is On
Robert Palmer - Simply Irresistible
Poison - Every Rose Has Its Thorn
Duran Duran - A View to a Kill
Prince - Raspberry Beret
Elton John - Candle in the Wind
Cyndi Lauper - Good Enough (The Goonies)
Céline Dion - Where Does My Heart Beat Now?
Madonna - Like a Virgin
Weird Al Yankovic - Like a Surgeon
Cher - I Found Someone
Kylie Minogue - Locomotion A handy dandy Spotify playlist for this collection: https://open.spotify.com/playlist/0aUVNGPFAuKN2MiEyHTR2K Wayback in 1973 - https://imgur.com/gallery/2rlT8Yh Wayback in 1983 - https://imgur.com/gallery/T1CGaYx Wayback in 1993 - https://imgur.com/gallery/tuAN3R4 Wayback in 1998 - https://imgur.com/gallery/2qki0gA

Let me know if you have any other questions! Have a great day all :D -Yuzu Music : BMTH - limousine

item.title: In China you can’t have 3 people wearing the same shirt
item.dc:creator: GlobalImageServiceCat

item.title: When you think you finally have a date.
item.description: Woman in my massage therapy class asked me out, talked for a few weeks while she was planning a date (her idea), and she admitted today that she doesn’t want to do it anymore because I have a collection of stuffed animals that are on a shelf in my bedroom. I was feeling quite excited since I basically never get matches on dating sites, so I’m feeling pretty bummed out and a bit like a loser. Still have to see her class every week for 5 more months too. F. FP EDIT: wow, didn't expect this to get that much attention. For the record I'm not looking for people to roast this person, I like her a person and we're going to interact for the foreseeable future. I'm just trying to figure out why this whole situation is making me feel like shit. 2nd edit: been asked a few times so here is the collection: https://imgur.com/gallery/y7MtRLO TLDR: Not mad at lady, why do I feel like shit?
item.dc:creator: saeheria

item.title: Why the trump campaign didn't release aerial shots of his S. Bronx rally

item.title: Roll Call - remnants and reflections of days long passed
item.description:
Roll Call (a perspective from those left behind): In the military, there is a ritual for almost every major transition in both career and life. The traditions and rites of passage practiced and passed down within the branches of our military serve almost as time capsules which contain remnants and reflections of days long passed. There is an antiquity to them which also carries with it a unifying element which elicits a kind of nostalgia in anyone who witnesses or participates in them. To name a few: for a pilot in the Air Force there are dollar rides, namings, "Mach tuck", and fini flights which serve as landmarks as they move through their career. And then there’s the missing man and the piano burns for when they die. And though there is a vast difference of traditions amongst the various communities, one that is common across most of them is the roll call.
A roll call can take many forms: There is the standard roll call which is very similar to what a teacher does when taking attendance. A formality that carries with it very little emotional importance. But then there are the roll calls which happen in a squadron bar. These are the ones that I would hear story after raucous story about. I always thought that squadron roll calls were nothing more than an excuse to gather and drink; all I knew was that the day after one, John ["Trojan" Graziano] would have nothing but murky details to share and a hangover to nurse. He’d describe them as best he could: the pilots would gather together, their call signs would be called out, and they would respond. A call and response. (And then…they would drink. And sing. And tell stories. And drink some more)
It wasn’t until John one day mentioned that the names of his deceased friends were also called out during roll call that the tradition began to carry with it a new resonance which even I as a civilian could appreciate. There was something about the vision of an entire squadron listening for their fallen friend’s name, and then sitting together in the brief silence or the “here-here’s” that followed that was profoundly moving. (This moment would of course be followed by drinking. And singing. And the telling of stories all the while drinking some more) The exact structure of a roll call varies across military branches: Sometimes the deceased’s name is called three times, sometimes just once. Sometimes it is their rank followed by their full name. Other times, in the case of squadron roll calls: it is simply the fallen pilot’s call sign.
Trojan, Bruiser, Rip, Cools, Doc, Chex… Despite the differences, there is one consistent detail that all roll calls share: no matter how many times a fallen soldier’s name is called, or what name they are actually called by, their voice will never be the one to respond back to it. The one consistent detail is that, for a split second, all of the deceased’s friends sit in that moment and exist in it without them. And then they drink… And they sing… And drink some more… So yes, the roll call is just a part of what takes place in the middle of a rowdy squadron, and yes the moment where their friend’s name hovers in the air before quickly evaporating into waves of squadron songs and tall tales is short lived, but their name was spoken nonetheless. Saying their name is important, even if they can’t say it back. Their name and all the energy and stories threaded into it is added to the air the entire squadron breathes for one night. That’s why when you ask someone how to honor one of their fallen friends on Memorial Day, the most common response is: just say their name. So this Memorial Day, do just that. In the middle of whatever bar-b-q, bar, or pool party you end up at, in between the songs and the drinking and the story telling, take a page out of a flying squadron’s playbook…and say their names.
Lieutenant Colonel Bryant was one of eight Airmen killed in the April 27, 2011 green on blue attack at the Kabul International Airport in Afghanistan. From Tennessee, Frank Bryant, Jr. was a high school state champion wrestler. He attended the U.S. Air Force Academy, where in 1995, he captained the wrestling team and was picked as the most valuable player on the team. After graduation he went to pilot training and upon receiving his wings taught as a T-37 instructor pilot. He then transitioned to the F-16 and flew it for the rest of his career, in which, he accumulated a total of 3,047 flying hours In 2003, his first deployment was in support of Operation Iraqi Freedom. He also served as an exchange pilot to the United Arab Emirates. While at Luke AFB, Bryant flew with the 63d Fighter Squadron and later served on the 56th Operations Group staff. In 2008, he was named the Nineteenth Air Force Air-to-Ground Fighter Pilot Instructor of the Year award, and chosen as the Nineteenth Air Force Top Instructor Fighter Pilot, for which he received the Gen. William R. Looney, III Award. Later, he deployed to Afghanistan as an air advisor to the 438th Air Expeditionary Advisor Group in Kabul, Afghanistan, as part of the Combined Joint Chiefs of Staff Afghanistan-Pakistan Hands Program. During his two deployments, he flew a total of 121 combat hours. On April 27, 2011, a single gunman, an Afghan pilot, opened fire on a small room of Airmen. The shooter later died from his wounds. Lieutenant Colonel Bryant was survived by his wife and young son. On May 20, 2011, Colonel Bryant was buried at Arlington National Cemetery.
Third from the right: Nicholas "Scooter" Hamilton. This is our air advisor team working with Iraqi F-16 pilots. Scooter was the lead advisor before he went back to Nellis and soon retire. After taking a contractor job flying Mirage F-1s, he suffered an inflight emergency and tragically didn't survive the ejection.
Attached is a photo of the Air Force F-35 initial cadre test pilots. Their Lightning number represents the order that they flew the airplane. From left to right, Scott "Shark" McLaren, Lightning 14, Jay "Sherman" Kearl, Lightning 18, Hank "Hog" Griffiths, Lightning 10, Matt "Rip" Hayden, Lightning 12, Steven "Whitey" Speares, Lightning 20 and Vince "Ice" Caterina, Lightning 23. The initial cadre are all standing in front of F-35 tail AF-2, that bears Rip's name. The loss of Rip last Sunday has been very tough on all of us, and it has taken me a while to post about this. When I was tapped to be the squadron commander of the 461st, the Air Force's first F-35 squadron, I was blessed with the opportunity to pick my team. Rip was the first pilot I chose. His reputation was stellar and many senior leaders were telling me to go meet this outstanding pilot and talk to him. We met for the first time at the Society of Experimental Test Pilots Symposium in Anaheim. Rip was extremely humble, I had to get him to brag about his accomplishments and tell me more about himself. He was an easy choice to be the Air Force's second pilot to fly the production representative F-35. He was there from the beginning, and was our lead flight sciences pilot and flew a majority of the loads test points in his machine, AF-2. During his tenure at the F-35 Integrated Test Force, he was instrumental in flying the airplane to where it had never been before, expanding the flight envelope, improving the jet's flying and handling qualities and molding it into the operational fighter it is today. These aren't just words to glorify a pilot who has passed, he was praised and recognized for his excellence. He was the Air Force Materiel Command's nominee for the Bobby Bond and Koren Kolligan AF level awards, won an Air Force Flight Test Center safety award for saving a test jet that had a critical design flaw during wet runway testing, he was the first F-35 Command Chief Pilot and the first Air Force instructor pilot in the F-35. Rip died doing what he loved, flying airplanes, but he was taken from us way too early and he had a lot more to give to aviation as the Chief Test Pilot for Sherman Kearl's flight test company. He will truly be missed. ~ Col (Ret) Hank "Hog" Griffiths
“So here’s a nickel on the grass to you, my friend, and your spirit, enthusiasm, sacrifice and courage – but most of all to your friendship. Yours is a dying breed and when you are gone, the world will be a lesser place.” A toast: Frank "Bruiser" Bryant, Jr Matt "Rip" Hayden Ben "Chex" Meier Eric "Doc" Schultz David "Cools" Cooley James "Crush" Duricy Nicholas "Scooter" Hamilton David "Klepto" Brodeur Aaron "Amber" Frey Will “Pyro” DuBois John “Trojan” Graziano https://www.airforcetimes.com/news/your-air-force/2021/08/10/draken-f1-fleet-returns-to-flight-after-fatal-crash-at-nellis/ https://www.arnold.af.mil/News/Article-Display/Article/3583047/f-15c-eagle-display-at-arnold-afb-honors-duricy/ https://www.alumni.caltech.edu/techer-news/in-memoriam-lt-col-eric-doc-schultz https://www.pasadenastarnews.com/2017/12/29/air-force-pilot-altadena-native-to-be-honored-by-rose-parade-flyover/
item.dc:creator: TheDrunkenBulldog

item.title: NAMES Project AIDS Memorial Quilt
item.description: AIDS Memorial Quilt photo: Public Domain AIDS Memorial Quilt on display at the National Mall in Washington, D.C. By National Institutes of Health - en wikipedia - [1], taken from National Institutes of Health website - http://aidshistory.nih.gov/tip_of_the_iceberg/quilt.html, Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=2685602  Sauce: Lynne Yamaguchi Fletcher, The First Gay Pope and Other Records (Boston, MA: Alyson Publications, Inc., 1992), pp. 42, 46. Retold in my own words to avoid plagiarism. “The NAMES Project AIDS Memorial Quilt, often abbreviated to AIDS Memorial Quilt or AIDS Quilt, is a memorial to celebrate the lives of people who have died of AIDS-related causes. Weighing an estimated 54 tons, it is the largest piece of community folk art in the world as of 2020. It was conceived in 1985, during the early years of the AIDS pandemic, when social stigma prevented many AIDS victims from receiving funerals. It has been displayed on the Mall in Washington, D.C., several times. In 2020, it returned to San Francisco, where it is now cared for by the National AIDS Memorial. It can be seen virtually.” https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NAMES_Project_AIDS_Memorial_Quilt The History of the Quilt https://www.aidsmemorial.org/quilt-history Video Essay the AIDS Memorial Quilt: Origins, Legacy, Futures https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7HsDs3YRRZg
item.dc:creator: oukid

item.title: Animals on social media.
item.dc:creator: samirs9791337

item.title: I am the WORST PERSON
item.description: It’s time for me to head to bed. I’ve got a big day tomorrow, and need my sleep. But these little bastards are preventing me from leaving the kitten room. I picked them up a few hours ago and they have already attached themselves to me. I mean. Come ON man. Look at them!! They’re all stretchy and snuggly and purring. It’s not even fair. I have to go!! I hope they forgive me.
item.dc:creator: KittensForDays
